summary: I was living with my parents in Lyons, Illinois when I was drafted in March 1969. Although I lived in Lyons, I actually went to high school in Berwyn. Lyons was my home base during those years before I entered the Army.

Before entering the service, I worked in Cicero, Illinois at a bindery where we did the final work on printed materials. My brother, who was a foreman there, helped me get the job after I graduated high school in 1966. Cicero was where I spent my civilian working days before being drafted.

I attended Morton West High School in Berwyn, Illinois, even though I lived in Lyons. I graduated from there in 1966. It was a public high school where many of my friends were either enlisted or joined the service around the time I was drafted.

I was drafted into the United States Army in March 1969 and served about a year and a half. My older brother had enlisted in the Army in 1966 and served three years, so there was a family tradition of military service. The Army sent me to basic training at Fort Leonard Wood and then medic training at Fort Sam Houston before deploying me to Vietnam.

My family has a history connected to World War II; my older brother served in the occupation forces in Germany after the war, and two of my uncles also served during that time. Although I was drafted during the Vietnam War, the legacy of World War II service was part of my family's background.
cues: older brother in occupation in Germany

summary: My older brother was stationed in Germany as part of the occupation forces after World War II. That experience was part of my family's military history and influenced our views on service. Although I never served there, Germany was a significant place in my family's past.

After being inducted in Chicago, I was flown to Fort Leonard Wood in Missouri for my basic training. The training lasted about eight weeks, and it was my first real experience in the Army. I remember it as a time of confusion and adjustment, with lots of testing and preparation.

Following basic training, I was sent to Fort Sam Houston near San Antonio, Texas, for medic training. The training lasted about ten weeks during the hot summer months of 1969. It was tough and fast-paced, and I had no prior medical experience before being assigned there.

San Antonio was where Fort Sam Houston is located, and that's where I completed my medic training in 1969. The summer heat was intense, sometimes reaching 100 degrees during the day and still very warm at night. It was a challenging environment but an important part of my preparation for Vietnam.

I was drafted during the Vietnam War and deployed there in September 1969. The war was winding down when I got out, which allowed me to apply for an early discharge after about a year and a half. My time in Vietnam was marked by the tropical heat, the challenges of being a medic, and the realities of war.

I was assigned to the United States Army Medical Department as a medic, even though I had no prior medical background. The Army sent me to Fort Sam Houston for ten weeks of medic training before deploying me to Vietnam. Being a medic was a tough job, and I felt the training was rushed given the realities we faced in the field.

During basic training, one of the drill instructors who stood out was Sergeant Walters, the chief drill instructor. He had served two tours in Vietnam and often shared stories about the war. I remember he used the French word 'beaucoup' and explained it as Vietnamese, which was actually a French influence from the colonial period.

The French colonial empire's influence was still evident in Vietnam when I was there in 1969. For example, some Vietnamese expressions were actually French, like the word 'beaucoup' that Sergeant Walters mentioned. This historical connection was something I noticed during my time in Vietnam.

On the way to Vietnam, our flight stopped in Alaska as a refueling or crew change point. It was part of the long journey from the United States to Southeast Asia. Although we didn't stay long, it was one of the few places we landed before reaching Vietnam.

Our plane also stopped in Okinawa on the way to Vietnam in 1969. It was another brief stop during the long flight overseas. Okinawa was a familiar transit point for many soldiers heading to Vietnam at that time.

When I arrived in South Vietnam in September 1969, we landed at Long Binh. There was a replacement depot there where new arrivals stayed for a few days before being assigned to their units. It was my first real experience on Vietnamese soil, and the tropical heat was immediately noticeable.

Although I don't recall landing directly at Tan Son Nhut International Airport, it was the main airport near Saigon. Instead, we landed at Long Binh, which was nearby, and then processed through the replacement depot. Tan Son Nhut was a key location for many soldiers arriving in Vietnam.

I don't recall being assigned directly to the 9th Infantry Division, but after processing at Long Binh, soldiers like me were sent to various units. The 9th Infantry Division was one of the active units in Vietnam at the time. My role as a medic meant I could have been attached to infantry units, but I was not specifically assigned to that division.
